coredata.dc:title: Inhibition of UV-B stress in lettuce through enzyme-like Scutellaria baicalensis carbon dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Inc.

coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Hui
Kang, Yunyan
Yang, Ni
Li, Hui
Huang, Sirui
Liang, Zishan
Zeng, Guiling
Huang, Yi
Li, Wei
Zheng, Mingtao
Huang, Riming
Lei, Bingfu
Yang, Xian
coredata.dc:description: Oxidative stress in plants caused by UV-B stress has always been a great challenge to the yield of agricultural products. Carbon dots (CDs) with enzyme-like activity have been developed, and inhibiting oxidative stress in animals has been achieved, but little is known about abiotic stress resistance in plants, especially UV-B stress. In this study, CDs were synthesized from Scutellaria baicalensis via a hydrothermal method. The ability of CDs to scavenge reactive oxygen species (ROS) in vivo and in vitro and to enhance antioxidant resistance in vivo was evaluated. The results show that CDs promoted the nutrient assimilation ability of lettuce seedlings and protected the plants from UV-B stress by increasing the activities of superoxide dismutase (SOD), peroxidase (POD), catalase (CAT), glutathione reductase (GR), and ascorbate peroxidase (APX). Moreover, the antioxidant metabolism of plants can be activated by CDs and the expression levels of aquaporin (AQP) genes PIP1 and PIP2 are also up-regulated. These results facilitate the design and fabrication of CDs to meet the challenge of abiotic stress in food production.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Enzyme-like carbon dots
Oxidative stress
Antioxidation
Free radical scavenging

Visualization of free radical scavenging in vivo
Oxidative stress inhibition in lettuce seedling leaves
In vitro assessment of the antioxidizability of CDs
Visualization of the free radical scavenging effect in vivo
CDs protect lettuce from UV-B stress

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) (b) HRTEM image of the prepared CDs. (c) FT-IR spectrum and full XPS pattern (d) of CDs. (e) High-resolution XPS C 1 s and O 1 s (f) spectra of CDs and fitting results.

(a-c) Effect of different concentrations of CDs on the absorption of different reaction solutions (NBT, SA, and DPPH). Percent elimination of O2 •− (d), •OH(e), and DPPH• (f) at different concentrations of CDs. Absorption of NBT (g), salicylic acid (SA) (h), and DPPH (i) mixed with the CDs and ascorbic acid (Vc, as a reference). ** , *** represent significance at p < 0.01 and p < 0.001, respectively.
Histochemical detection of H2O2 (a) with DAB and of O2 •− (b) with NBT in leaves of lettuce treated with UV-B. (CK: with UV-B but without CDs; scale bars: 0.5 cm).

(a) Aerial fresh weight and dry weight (b) of lettuce seedlings treated with different concentrations of CDs, (c) net photosynthesis of lettuce seedlings, (d) plant morphology at the harvest period of lettuce seedlings after treatment with or without (CK) CDs (scale bars: 2 cm), and (e) Chlorophyll content of lettuce seedlings. Different letters indicate significant differences among different treatments at the p < 0.05 level.

Activity of SOD (a), POD (b), CAT (c), GR (d), and APX (e) in lettuce seedlings treated with different concentrations of CDs. (f) MDA content in seedlings treated with different concentrations of CDs. Different letters indicate significant differences among different treatments at the p < 0.05 level.
Expression levels of PIP1 (a) and PIP2 (b) in lettuce seedlings after treatment with or without CDs. *** represents a significant difference from the control p < 0.001.
Schematic illustration of the formation mechanism of the CDs.

head.ce:title: Inhibition of UV-B stress in lettuce through enzyme-like Scutellaria baicalensis carbon dots

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors have contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Oxidative stress in plants caused by UV-B stress has always been a great challenge to the yield of agricultural products. Carbon dots (CDs) with enzyme-like activity have been developed, and inhibiting oxidative stress in animals has been achieved, but little is known about abiotic stress resistance in plants, especially UV-B stress. In this study, CDs were synthesized from Scutellaria baicalensis via a hydrothermal method. The ability of CDs to scavenge reactive oxygen species (ROS) in vivo and in vitro and to enhance antioxidant resistance in vivo was evaluated. The results show that CDs promoted the nutrient assimilation ability of lettuce seedlings and protected the plants from UV-B stress by increasing the activities of superoxide dismutase (SOD), peroxidase (POD), catalase (CAT), glutathione reductase (GR), and ascorbate peroxidase (APX). Moreover, the antioxidant metabolism of plants can be activated by CDs and the expression levels of aquaporin (AQP) genes PIP1 and PIP2 are also up-regulated. These results facilitate the design and fabrication of CDs to meet the challenge of abiotic stress in food production.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Mechanism diagram of enzyme-like activated CDs protecting lettuce from UV-B stress.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Global climate change will lead to environmental stressors could cause serious damage to crops and plants in the coming decades (Faseela and Puthur, 2018). Environmental damage directly leads to the enhancement of UV-B radiation on Earth, posing a threat to plant growth that cannot be ignored. Although stress is inevitable, it can be feasibly alleviated. When plants are under UV-B stress, a large number of reactive oxygen species (ROS), such as singlet oxygen (1O2), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), and superoxide anion radical (O2 •−), are produced in the plant, destroying the cell structure and physiological functions of plants and resulting in reduced production (Su et al., 2020). Therefore, removing excess ROS is very important to maintain the normal function of plants. However, the use of a large number of chemicals will aggravate environmental problems and cause concern about food safety (Sachdev et al., 2021; Gill and Tuteja, 2010). CDs is a kind of carbon-based nanomaterial which have attracted extensive attention in oxidation resistance and disease resistance because of their easy entry into plants, environmental friendliness, unique optical and chemical properties and low cost (Li et al., 2018; Su et al., 2018). In recent years, many reports have proven that CDs can improve the resistance of plants to abiotic stress (Chen et al., 2020; Kou et al., 2021b). To date, there are two strategies to achieve or improve the ROS scavenging activity of CDs: oxygen-containing functional groups (e.g., OC−OH) can facilitate the formation of adducts or electron transfer between ROS and CDs (Chong et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2020). The enzyme-like activity of CDs has attracted much attention. Fan et al. (2018) synthesized nitrogen-doped nanoenzymes with enzyme-like activity to treat tumours. The CDs prepared by Dong et al. can inhibit oxidative stress in vivo (Dong et al., 2021). However, little is known about the application of CDs with enzyme-like activity to abiotic stress. We are committed to synthesizing CDs with low cost and suitable enzyme-like activity to improve plant resistance.

At this time, researchers have given much attention to traditional Chinese medicines because it entails natural raw materials, has a large output and causes almost no harm (Luo et al., 2021; Ma et al., 2019). More importantly, Chinese herbal medicines are rich in active ingredients and have a variety of pharmacological substances (Teschke et al., 2015; Li and Wang, 2017), which can achieve ideal results without drug loading or element doping (Li and Weng, 2017), not only meeting the urgent needs of large-scale production of CDs but also promoting their sustainable application (Zhang et al., 2017). In previous reports, some traditional Chinese medicine were used as raw materials to prepare functional CDs due to their unique efficacy (Sun et al., 2018; Servick, 2020). The above results inspired us to identify a kind of traditional Chinese medicine with good antioxidant activity as a precursor for functionalized CDs development.

Scutellaria baicalensis, a very common, low-cost traditional Chinese medicine, has a wide range of pharmacological effects and clinical applications (Thi et al., 2021). Flavonoids, such as baicalein and wogonin (Gong et al., 2014), are the main active compounds of S. baicalensis, so it has good anticancer (Orzechowska et al., 2020), antioxidant (Arweiler et al., 2011), and anti-inflammatory effects (Ban et al., 2011). In addition, this plant is rich in carbonyl groups, making it an ideal carbon material for preparing enzyme-like activity CDs (Jiao et al., 2020).

Therefore, we used S. baicalensis as a material to synthesize a new type of CDs with enzyme-like activity by a one-step hydrothermal method. Additionally, the free radical scavenging effect of the developed CDs was evaluated in vitro. On this basis, the tolerance of lettuce seedlings to UV-B stress after CDs treatment was evaluated. CDs were synthesized by "green" precursors and used in green plants to obtain "green" agricultural products, providing a method for the efficient production of eco-friendly and safe vegetables in harsh environments.

CDs were synthesized by the one-pot hydrothermal method with S. baicalensis. Briefly, 15 g of ground powder of dried S. baicalensis was first mixed in 300 mL of deionized water, the solution was then sonicated for 20 min until well mixed, and the mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and reacted at 180 °C for 6 h. The resulting mixture was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min to remove the precipitate. The supernatant was purified on a 0.22 µm filter membrane, lyophilised and stored at 4 °C until needed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Free radical scavenging activities in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: Three typical free radicals (O2 •−, hydroxyl radicals (•OH), DPPH radicals (DPPH•)) were used to assess the in vitro scavenging activity of CDs. The removal rate of O2 •− was measured by the nitroblue tetrazolium chloride (NBT) photoreduction method (Li et al., 2020a). The •OH scavenging activity was measured by the Fenton reaction (Li et al., 2020b). The scavenging activity of DPPH• was assayed according to the method of Thaipong et al. (2006). The specific experimental methods are described in the Supporting Information.

The plant growth and cultivation experiments were carried out in the plant factory (SCAU Main Campus Teaching & Research Base) with a temperature of 25 ± 2 °C, a relative humidity of 60 % and a 12/12 h day/night regime. The growing substrate was Hoagland’s nutrient solution. Two-week-old Italian lettuce (Lactuca sativa L. var. ramosa Hort) seedlings was used to study the effects of CDs on plant growth. The seedlings were treated with UV-B fluorescent light for 12 h a day from 8 a.m. to 8 p.m. The radiation dose was tuned by adjusting the distance between the lamp and the seedling leaves. According to the method of Flint and Caldwell (2003) the total dose of bioavailable ultraviolet radiation in this study was 18 kJ•m−2•day−1. CDs (0, 0.5, 1, and 2 mg/mL) were applied with a small sprayer at 9 a.m. every day. Three replicates consisting of 12 seedlings were set up for each treatment. The control group was treated with distilled water. After 10 days of cotreatment with UV-B and CDs, treated leaves were collected and stored at − 80 °C for subsequent physiological parameter analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visualization of free radical scavenging in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: H2O2 and O2 •− in leaves were visualized with some modifications to the method of Chao et al. (2011). Briefly, fresh, fully expanded seedling leaves were stained with 1 % 3,3-dimethoxybenzidine (DAB) or 0.25 mM NBT for 10 h at room temperature. After incubation in DAB or NBT, the leaves were washed with distilled water and then decolorized in boiling 95 % (v/v) ethanol so that dark brown spots (for H2O2) or blue polymerization products (for O2 •−) could be detected.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Oxidative stress inhibition in lettuce seedling leaves
ce:section.ce:para: After all the seedlings were harvested, we measured the relevant physiological indices. The chlorophyll content was determined according to Porra et al. (1989). A photosynthesis system (Li-Cor 6400; Li-Cor, Inc., Nebraska, USA) was used to monitor the net photosynthetic rate. SOD activity was measured by absorbance at 560 nm after NBT photoreduction. POD activity was measured by the absorbance increase rate of a UV–vis spectrophotometer at 470 nm for 3 min. The activity of CAT was determined by determining the decomposition rate of hydrogen peroxide at 240 nm wavelength for 2 min. The malondialdehyde (MDA) content was obtained by colorimetric analysis of the reaction solution at 600, 532, and 450 nm. The APX activity was determined by measuring the oxidation rate of ascorbic acid at 290 nm for 40 s. GR activity was calculated by measuring the rate of NADPH dehydrogenation at 340 nm (Nakano and Asada, 1981; Foyer and Halliwell, 1976).

The RNA of lettuce was extracted by an RNA assay kit and reverse transcribed by a cDNA Synthesis Kit (Tsingke Biological Co., China). The extracted RNA was measured by an ultramicro spectrophotometer. The purity and concentration of RNA are qualified RNA detected by subsequent reverse transcription. SYBR Green I (Tsingke Biological Co., China) was used for amplification quantitative PCR. Gene expression was calculated by the 2−ΔΔC T method. Gene primers are shown in Table S1.

SPSS 2017 (SPSS Inc., Chicago, IL, USA) was used for data (mean ± SD, n = biological replicates) analysis. Comparison between treatments was performed by independent samples t-test (two tailed) or one-way ANOVA based on Duncan’s multiple range test (two tailed). Different letters indicate significance at p < 0.05. * , **, *** represent significance at p < 0.05, p < 0.01, and p < 0.001, respectively.

Scheme 1 shows a schematic illustration of the formation mechanism of the CDs, and the yield was about 38 %. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of CDs are uniform in shape with an average diameter (2.67 nm) ( Fig. 1 a), and we can observe that the lattice spacing is 0.21 nm in the high-resolution TEM image of CDs (Fig. 1 b). It is clear that CDs emit blue luminescence at 365 nm excitation, and a previous study demonstrated that the blue luminescence of CDs contributed to the CO, CN, and C−N groups (Ding et al., 2014). To clarify the origin of the blue emission of the as-prepared CDs, Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) and X-ray spectra were acquired. As shown in Fig. 1 c, FT-IR spectroscopy was performed on KBr pellets, which showed the presence of characteristic peaks at 3384 cm−1 (νs = O−H), 2925 cm−1 (νs = sp2 = C−H), 1606 cm−1 (νs = CC), 1403 cm−1 (νs = C−O), 1072 and 1019 cm−1 (C−O− C) (Li et al., 2016). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed to determine the elemental composition and surface functional state of CDs (Fig. 1 d). The CDs mainly consisted of C (284.8 eV, at% = 64.55), N (399.5 eV, at% = 3.47), and O (532.4 eV, at% = 31.98) element. In Fig. 1 e, the C 1 s spectrum can be fitted with three peaks at 284.8, 286.3, and 287.6 eV, which can be assigned to C−C, C−O, and CO, respectively. The O 1 s spectrum proves the existence of CO (531.3 eV) and C−O (532.8 eV) bonds (Fig. 1 f), respectively (Wang et al., 2021). The above FT-IR and XPS spectra indicated that the CDs were functionalized (luminescence and removal of ROS) with –OH and CO.

Furthermore, we investigated the optical properties of CDs. The UV–vis absorption peaks at 265 nm are ascribed to π-π * (Fig. S2) (Lim et al., 2015). The fluorescence spectrum of the CDs found to be the best excitation is 377 nm and the best emission is 477 nm (Fig. S3). The emission spectra of CDs show excitation-dependent emission characteristics from 357 nm to 397 nm under excitation. Fig. S4 shows that the emission peak gradually redshifts with increasing excitation wavelength, indicating the existence of different emission sites on the CD surface (Zhu et al., 2015).

We considered the biocompatibility of CDs before using CDs in plants, so we evaluated their cytotoxicity by the CCK8 method. Compared with plant cells, animal cells do not contain cell walls and absorb nanoparticles more easily. Therefore, animal cells are more sensitive to CDs than plant cells (Li et al., 2020b). As shown in Fig. S1, the survival rate of HeLa cells was above 82 % in all groups after treatment with different concentrations of CDs. These data suggested that CDs were biocompatible and could be used in plants.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro assessment of the antioxidizability of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Based on previous studies, we know that nanoparticles have the ability to scavenge free radicals (Kou et al., 2021b; Kasouni et al., 2021). In this experiment, the scavenging activities of three free radicals were evaluated. As shown in Fig. 2 a, b, and c, the addition of CDs causes a significant decrease in the absorption of the reaction solution, which proves that the O2 •−, •OH, and DPPH• scavenging capacities all increase with increasing CD concentration and are dose dependent. Similarly, Fig. 2 d, e, and f shows that the highest O2 •−, •OH, and DPPH• scavenging activities of CDs were 50 %, 73 %, and 75 %, respectively, at a concentration of 2 mg/mL. Additionally, we used ascorbic acid as a reference to evaluate the scavenging effect (Fig. 2 g, h, and i). It was interesting to observe that the •OH and DPPH• radical scavenging capacity of CDs was better than that of ascorbic acid. These results indicated that CDs had excellent enzyme-like activity. Combined with the results of previous studies, we know, on the one hand, CDs are excellent electron donors and acceptors, and then they can scavenge free radicals through electron transfer (Dong et al., 2021). On the one hand, when −OH and CO coexist, they can work together to degrade free radicals (Li et al., 2021). Our survey of some previously reported studies on ROS scavenging inferred that most CDs have the ability to scavenge anionic radicals, and the scavenging ability for the full range of radicals remains to be investigated (Table S1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visualization of the free radical scavenging effect in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: When plants are subjected to UV-B stress, excess ROS are produced in the plant (Asada, 2006). Staining allows for the visualization of ROS aggregation within the leaf, thus providing initial insight into the effect of CDs. After UV-B treatment in the leaves of lettuce, the presence of excess H2O2 and O2 •− can be clearly seen by the examination of dark brown spots and blue polymerization products on leaves without CD treatment ( Fig. 3 a and b). In contrast, the H2O2 and O2 •− accumulation in lettuce leaves after CDs treatment decreased significantly. The surface of CDs prepared in this study was rich in −OH, so CDs transferred free electrons from free radicals to the C-C skeleton in the carbon core, so that the free radicals were scavenged. (Chong et al., 2016). Combined with the study of free radical scavenging activity in vitro, CDs can also scavenge ROS in plants. These results are consistent with those of previous literature reports (Zhang et al., 2018).

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs protect lettuce from UV-B stress
ce:section.ce:para: According to the results of the preexperiment, 0.75 mg/mL CDs-treated plants were notably tolerant to UV-B stress (Fig. S5). To explore the optimum concentration for agricultural production, in the follow-up experiment, a concentration gradient was set up above and below this concentration to explore the impact of CDs on alleviating UV-B stress in lettuce. In this study, we treated lettuce seedlings with UV-B and CDs for 10 days to obtain a model of oxidative damage in plants. First, we studied the impacts of CDs on the growth and photosynthesis of lettuce seedlings, which were measured by foliar spraying. In Fig. 4 a and b, after spraying CDs, the biomass of lettuce seedlings increased rather than decreased, although they were damaged by UV-B, especially at 0.5 mg/mL, compared with CK (0 mg/mL), the fresh and dry weight of lettuce seedlings increased by 132.9 % and 277.2 %, respectively, which was consistent with the phenotypic diagram (Fig. 4 d). Similarly, the net photosynthesis and chlorophyll content of lettuce seedlings all showed similar trends. Under UV-B irradiation, the net photosynthetic rate and chlorophyll content of leaves in the 0.5 mg/mL CDs treatment group increased significantly by 15.6 % and 39.9 % compared with the CK, respectively (Fig. 4 e). In addition, we measured the relative growth rate (RGR) of lettuce under UV-B stress with and without 0.5 mg/mL CDs treatment. RGR is an indicator of the extent to which a species uses its photosynthetic products for growth and is influenced by environmental factors (Puglielli et al., 2017). The results showed that the RGR of lettuce was significantly increased by 48.9 % after treatment with appropriate concentrations of CDs, which also provided good evidence of the effect of CDs on the photosynthetic rate of lettuce under stress (Fig. S6). The above data demonstrate that CDs play a crucial role against the toxic effect of UV-B in lettuce plant. Combined with previous relevant literature our analysis shows that due to the photoluminescence characteristics of CDs (Li et al., 2020b), CDs can transfer energy to chloroplasts after being stimulated by light, accelerating the rate of photosynthetic electron transfer, so despite being in a stress environment, the photosynthetic activity of chloroplasts was improved. Similarly, Chen et al. (2020) used N-doped carbon dots in Arabidopsis thaliana which was poisoned by Cd, and the biomass was also improved compared with the control.

Furthermore, to evaluate the inhibitory oxidative stress effect in vivo, we studied the antioxidant status that consists of SOD, POD, CAT, GR, APX, and MDA. SOD is a major O2 •− scavenger, and its enzymatic action leads to the generation of H2O2 and O2 •−, POD can reduce hydrogen peroxide, and CAT plays a crucial role in catalysing the decomposition of H2O2 (Dong et al., 2021). Our data showed that treatment with CDs at different concentrations increased the activities of SOD, POD, and CAT, especially at a concentration of 0.5 mg/mL, and the activities of SOD, POD, and CAT increased by 57.0 %, 35.9 %, and 44.5 % compared with the CK, respectively ( Fig. 5 a, b, and c). Similarly, GR and APX are biological enzymes that degrade peroxides and are widely found in plants (Dai et al., 2021). In Fig. 5 d and e, GR levels were increased by CDs, thus preventing UV-B stress-induced oxidative damage. Plants produce a large amount of ROS after stress, which aggravates the degree of membrane lipid peroxidation, which is manifested by an increase in MDA levels (Ozkur et al., 2009). Compared with the CK, the different concentrations of CDs resulted in a significant reduction in MDA levels in all treatment groups of lettuce (Fig. 5 f). The results of this study indicate that CDs play an important role in promoting plant resistance to UV-B stress. After analysis, the following two reasons have been summarized: (1) from the previous characterization of CDs, we know that the surface of CDs is rich in carboxyl groups, which may relieve the oxidative damages by improving the activity of antioxidase (Athanasia et al., 2021); (2) CDs enhanced the antioxidant defence system of plants. Similar to the present study, CDs have been shown in previous studies to protect plants from salt stress by improving antioxidant activity defence system of plants (Li et al., 2020a).

Aquaporins (AQP) are proteins located in the cell membrane that play a key role in water transport under normal and stressful conditions. The role of AQP in maintaining or restoring water homeostasis under abiotic stresses has been demonstrated (Kou et al., 2021b). Based on this, we hypothesized that the ability of CDs to promote lettuce resistance to UV-B stress might also be related to the enhancement of water channel protein genes. To clarify this point, we determined the effect of CDs on AQP genes, and in this study, we examined the expression of lettuce PIP1 ( Fig. 6 a) and PIP2 (Fig. 6 b). The results showed that all the above genes were significantly expressed under UV-B stress after CDs treatment compared to the plants without CDs. This result supported our speculation and was consistent with the response of total fresh weight and RWC (relative water content) (Fig. S7) to CDs. Thus, the hydrophilic groups, such as −OH and −COOH, contained in the CDs allow for a much higher expression of the AQP gene, thus protecting the plant from stress (Kou et al., 2021a).

In summary, based on the excellent antioxidant properties of S. baicalensis and excellent properties of CDs, we have developed a kind of CDs with high enzyme-like activity. In vitro, the CDs showed excellent scavenging against various free radicals. In vivo, CDs not only effectively reduced the accumulation of ROS and promoted the antioxidant system but also increased the expression of AQP genes and the enzyme activity of plants like the activity of SOD, POD, CAT and so on, thereby activating antioxidant activity and inhibiting oxidative damage. The present study suggested that the ability of CDs to combat oxidative stress in vivo and in vitro was based on their enzyme-like activity. These results made us have a more environmentally friendly and low-cost option in the selection of plant protectants so that will help us cope with food safety challenges caused by environmental degradation.

H.W: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Y.K: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. N.Y: Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. H.L: Writing – review & editing. S.H: Writing – review & editing. Z.L: Writing – review & editing G.Z: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Y.H: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. W.L: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. M.Z: Writing – review & editing. R.H: Writing – review & editing. B.L: Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition. X.Y: Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by the Guangdong Provincial Special Fund for Modern Agriculture Industry Technology Innovation Teams (No. 2022KJ122 and 2023KJ122), the National Natural Science Foundations of China (No. 12274144), the Independent Research and Development Projects of Maoming Laboratory (No. 2021ZZ004), the Guangdong Provincial Science & Technology Project (No. 2021B0707010003), the Guangzhou Science & Technology Project (No. 202103000059), and the Project of GDUPS (2018) for Prof. Bingfu LEI.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.ecoenv.2022.114177.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Arweiler et al., 2011

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Clinical and antibacterial effect of an anti-inflammatory toothpaste formulation with Scutellaria baicalensis extract on experimental gingivitis
Clin. Oral. Investig.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Arweiler, N.B., Pergola, G., Kuenz, J., Hellwig, E., Sculean, A., Auschill, T.M., 2011. Clinical and antibacterial effect of an anti-inflammatory toothpaste formulation with Scutellaria baicalensis extract on experimental gingivitis. Clin. Oral Investig. 15, 909–913.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Asada, 2006

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Production and scavenging of reactive oxygen species in chloroplasts and their functions
Plant Physiol.
